comment_text.snp5.7:1.4: At Kaṭha Upaniṣad 2.17, the ultimate “support” is the syllable om.
comment_text.snp5.7:2.3: Natthīti abbreviates natthi kiñcī’ti, which marks the dimension of nothingness.
comment_text.snp5.7:2.4: Niddesa explains kathāhi as either “doubts” (truncated kathaṅkathā), or “talks”. Elsewhere (paṭi)virato is commonly used with speech and never, to my knowledge, with doubt.
comment_text.snp5.7:3.4: Accept text’s vimutto against Niddesa’s (a)dhimutto (Cnd 10:22.2). Here, the meditator has already attained the dimension of nothingness, which is the highest of the attainments “with perception” (SN 14.11:5.1). Adhimutta would imply that they are “intent on” realizing the next attainment, as for example MN 105:13.4.
comment_text.snp5.7:5.1: In this verse, tiṭṭhe, sītisiyā, and cavetha are all optatives.
comment_text.snp5.7:5.2: Pūga is explained by the Niddesa as thousands of years or eons, a meaning also found at Pv 42:9.2. The Bhāgavata Purāṇa has varṣa-pūga in the same sense (2.5.34, 4.12.43).
comment_text.snp5.7:6.3: Compare the stock idiom saṅkhampi na upeti at eg. SN 20.2:1.7; also Snp 1.12:3.4. Also compare na pamāṇameti at Iti 69:4.3.
comment_text.snp5.7:6.4: Nāmakāya appears here and at DN 15:20.2 meaning the “set” of mental phenomena known as “name”.

root_text.snp5.7:7.4: Tathā hi te vidito esa dhammo”.
root_text.snp5.7:8.3: Yena naṁ vajjuṁ taṁ tassa natthi;

translation_text.snp5.7:1.3: “I am not able to cross the great flood.
translation_text.snp5.7:1.5: depending on which I may cross this flood.”
translation_text.snp5.7:2.3: depending on the perception ‘there is nothing’, <j>cross the flood.
translation_text.snp5.7:2.4: Giving up sensual pleasures, <j>refraining from chatter,
translation_text.snp5.7:2.5: watch day and night for the ending of craving.”
translation_text.snp5.7:3.1: “One who is free of desire for sensual pleasuress,”
translation_text.snp5.7:3.3: “depending on nothingness, all else left behind,
translation_text.snp5.7:3.4: freed in the ultimate liberation of perception:
translation_text.snp5.7:3.5: might they remain there without travelling on?”
translation_text.snp5.7:4.1: “One free of desire for sensual pleasuress,”
translation_text.snp5.7:4.3: “depending on nothingness, all else left behind,
translation_text.snp5.7:4.4: freed in the ultimate liberation of perception:
translation_text.snp5.7:4.5: they might remain there without travelling on.”
translation_text.snp5.7:5.1: “If they were to remain there without travelling on,
translation_text.snp5.7:5.3: and, being freed, were to grow cool right there,
translation_text.snp5.7:5.4: would the consciousness of that sort pass away?”
translation_text.snp5.7:6.1: “As a flame tossed by a gust of wind,”
translation_text.snp5.7:6.3: “comes to an end and cannot be reckoned;
translation_text.snp5.7:6.4: so too, a sage freed from the set of mental phenomena
translation_text.snp5.7:6.5: comes to an end and cannot be reckoned.”
translation_text.snp5.7:7.1: “One who has disappeared—do they not exist?
translation_text.snp5.7:7.2: Or are they free from disease for eternity?
translation_text.snp5.7:7.4: for truly you understand this matter.”
translation_text.snp5.7:8.1: “One who has disappeared cannot be defined,”
translation_text.snp5.7:8.3: “They have nothing <j>by which others might describe them.
translation_text.snp5.7:8.4: When all things have been eradicated,
translation_text.snp5.7:8.5: eradicated, too, are all ways of speech.”

variant_text.snp5.7:6.1: Accī → acci (sya-all cnd5:385 [Pucchāniddesa/ Upasīvamāṇavapucchāniddesa]) | khittā → khittaṁ (sya-all); khitto (pts-vp-pli1)
